enrichment.short_description: ChartHop is a people analytics and workforce planning platform that turns HR data into live, auto-updating org charts, headcount plans, and compensation insights.
enrichment.seo_title: ChartHop Review 2026: Pricing, Features & Org Charts
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ChartHop is a people analytics platform for live org charts, headcount planning, and compensation. Compare 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ChartHop is a people analytics and workforce planning software platform founded in 2019 by Ian White, headquartered in New York City. The company has raised venture funding from investors including Andreessen Horowitz and Cox Enterprises and employs roughly 51-200 people. ChartHop's core product connects HR, payroll, performance, and headcount data into a single source of truth, rendering it as dynamic, interactive org charts that update automatically as employee records change, rather than the static, manually redrawn charts common in spreadsheets or slide decks. Beyond visualization, ChartHop offers modular add-ons for headcount planning, compensation management, performance reviews, engagement surveys, and goal tracking, letting HR and finance teams collaborate on workforce decisions using the same underlying data set. Pricing is usage-based and modular, billed per employee per month (PEPM): the required ChartHop Core module starts at $5 PEPM, and add-on modules such as Headcount Planning, HRIS, Compensation, and Performance are priced at roughly $3-4 PEPM each, so companies pay only for the modules they use. Large organizations can negotiate custom Enterprise pricing with dedicated support. ChartHop integrates with common HRIS, payroll, and applicant tracking systems to pull employee data automatically, and its dashboards apply AI-assisted analytics to surface trends in headcount growth, pay equity, and organizational structure. The platform targets mid-market companies and fast-scaling startups - typically 100 to a few thousand employees - that have outgrown manually maintained spreadsheets for tracking headcount and compensation cycles. HR leaders use ChartHop to plan and approve headcount requests, model reorganizations before they happen, and give managers self-service visibility into their own teams. Compensation teams use it to run pay equity analyses and manage compensation review cycles with structured approval workflows. Because org charts are generated live from underlying HR data rather than drawn by hand, they stay accurate as employees join, leave, or change roles - a frequently cited advantage over static charting tools. ChartHop is delivered purely as a web application with no on-premise deployment option, and most workflows require no code to configure, making it accessible to HR generalists rather than requiring dedicated administrators. Reviewers on sites like G2 and Capterra generally cite the ease of building org charts, the flexibility of custom fields and views, and responsive customer support as strengths, while noting that modular PEPM pricing can add up as more add-ons are layered on, and that advanced reporting customization has a learning curve for non-technical users. ChartHop competes most directly with tools like Lattice and Pave, as well as the native org-chart features of legacy HRIS platforms, differentiating itself through the depth and interactivity of its org-chart-first approach to people analytics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ChartHop is a people analytics and workforce planning platform founded in 2019 by Ian White and headquartered in New York City. It connects HR, payroll, performance, and headcount data into a single source of truth and renders it as a live, interactive org chart that updates automatically whenever employee records change.

The platform is built for mid-market companies and fast-scaling startups that have outgrown spreadsheets and slide-based org charts, giving HR, finance, and people leaders a shared, real-time view of the organization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Beyond dynamic org charts, ChartHop offers modular tools for headcount planning, compensation management and pay equity analysis, performance reviews, engagement surveys, and goal tracking, all built on the same underlying employee data set.

AI-powered dashboards surface trends in headcount growth and organizational structure, and integrations with HRIS, payroll, and applicant tracking systems keep employee data current without manual re-entry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ChartHop uses modular, per-employee-per-month (PEPM) pricing. The required Core module starts at $5 PEPM, and add-on modules such as Headcount Planning, HRIS, Compensation, and Performance are priced around $3-4 PEPM each, so total cost depends on which modules a company enables. Enterprise pricing is custom and quote-based.

pros: Org charts stay accurate automatically since they're generated live from HR data
Modular pricing lets companies pay only for the modules they actually use
Highly customizable fields and views for different reporting needs
Strong fit for compensation planning and pay equity analysis
cons: Costs can add up quickly as more add-on modules are enabled
Advanced reporting and analytics have a learning curve for non-technical users
No self-hosted or on-premise deployment option
Best value is concentrated at mid-market scale rather than very small teams

faqs.question: What is ChartHop used for?
faqs.answer: ChartHop is used to build live org charts and to plan headcount, compensation, performance, and engagement using a single connected set of HR data.

faqs.question: How much does ChartHop cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing is modular and per-employee-per-month: the Core module starts at $5 PEPM, with add-on modules like Headcount Planning and Compensation priced around $3-4 PEPM each; Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Does ChartHop replace a full HRIS?
faqs.answer: ChartHop offers an HRIS add-on module, but many customers use it alongside an existing HRIS or payroll system via integrations rather than as a full replacement.

faqs.question: Who is ChartHop best suited for?
faqs.answer: Mid-market companies and fast-scaling startups, typically in the 100-to-several-thousand-employee range, that have outgrown spreadsheet-based org charts and headcount tracking.

faqs.question: Who founded ChartHop?
faqs.answer: ChartHop was founded in 2019 by Ian White and is headquartered in New York City.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of ChartHop?
faqs.answer: ChartHop does not publish a free tier; pricing is modular PEPM billing starting with the required Core module.

enrichment.short_description: ChartMogul is a subscription analytics platform that unifies billing data from Stripe, Chargebee, and other systems to automatically calculate MRR, churn, and SaaS metrics.
enrichment.seo_title: ChartMogul Pricing & Review 2026: SaaS Metrics Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ChartMogul tracks MRR, churn, and SaaS metrics from Stripe, Chargebee & more. See 2026 pricing plans, features, integrations, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ChartMogul is a subscription analytics and SaaS metrics platform founded in 2014 by Nick Franklin, a former Zendesk director, who launched the company as a solo founder after moving to Berlin, Germany. ChartMogul has raised $3.7 million in funding from investors including Point Nine Capital and Alven, and operates with a globally distributed team of roughly 57 people across hubs in Berlin, Toronto, and Seoul. The product connects to more than 25 billing and payment platforms - including Stripe, Chargebee, Recurly, Braintree, PayPal, and Chargify - to automatically calculate core subscription metrics such as Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR), Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR), customer lifetime value (LTV), average revenue per account (ARPA), and multiple churn rate variants. Because subscription businesses often run billing across several systems, ChartMogul's core value proposition is normalizing that fragmented data into one unified, real-time recurring-revenue view rather than requiring companies to build custom dashboards or spreadsheets. Beyond dashboards, ChartMogul offers customer and revenue segmentation, cohort analysis, plan-level breakdowns, and workflow automation that can trigger actions based on subscriber events such as trial-to-paid conversion or cancellation. It also connects to CRM and customer-data tools like HubSpot, Salesforce, Intercom, and Segment, and can push data into a customer's own data warehouse for further BI analysis. ChartMogul's pricing is tiered and scales with a company's ARR rather than being a flat monthly fee: the Free plan covers companies tracking up to $10,000 in MRR; Starter runs from roughly $59 to $699 per month depending on ARR and supports one connected billing system and up to three team members; Pro runs from roughly $99 to $1,199 per month, supports multiple billing systems in a unified view, two-way CRM sync, and unlimited team members; and Enterprise starts from $19,900 per year for companies exceeding $10 million in ARR, adding data-warehouse push and dedicated support. All paid plans include one complimentary CRM seat, with additional CRM seats billed at $39 per user per month, and annual billing carries a discount versus monthly billing. ChartMogul is aimed at SaaS and subscription-based businesses, from early-stage startups tracking their first few thousand dollars of MRR to larger, established recurring-revenue companies that need a single source of truth for board reporting and investor updates. Its benchmarking reports, drawn from aggregated and anonymized data across the SaaS companies on its platform, are widely cited across the industry as reference points for churn rates and growth benchmarks. Compared to building custom revenue dashboards in a BI tool, ChartMogul's advantage is that it ships with subscription-specific metric definitions already built in and reconciled across billing systems, reducing the engineering effort needed for accurate recurring-revenue reporting. It is a purely cloud-based SaaS product with no self-hosted option and offers a public API for custom integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ChartMogul is a subscription analytics platform founded in 2014 by Nick Franklin, a former Zendesk director. Headquartered in Berlin with additional hubs in Toronto and Seoul, the company helps subscription businesses unify billing data and measure recurring revenue accurately.

The platform connects to 25+ billing systems, including Stripe, Chargebee, and Recurly, so companies running billing across multiple tools can see a single, reconciled view of their recurring revenue rather than piecing together spreadsheets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ChartMogul automatically calculates SaaS metrics including MRR, ARR, churn (customer, revenue, and net MRR churn), LTV, and ARPA, and provides customer and revenue segmentation, cohort analysis, and workflow automation triggered by subscription events.
It also syncs with CRM tools like HubSpot and Salesforce, and can push data to a customer's own data warehouse for deeper BI analysis alongside its published SaaS benchmarking reports.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ChartMogul offers a free plan for companies under $10K MRR. Paid tiers scale with ARR: Starter runs $59-$699/month, Pro runs $99-$1,199/month, and Enterprise starts at $19,900/year for companies above $10M ARR. Additional CRM seats cost $39/user/month, and annual billing includes a discount.

cons: Pricing scales with ARR and can get expensive as a company grows

Some advanced features (multi-billing-system view, warehouse push) require Pro or Enterprise
Additional CRM seats are billed separately from the base plan

faqs.question: What is ChartMogul used for?
faqs.answer: ChartMogul is used to unify billing data and automatically calculate SaaS metrics like MRR, ARR, and churn for subscription businesses.

faqs.question: Is ChartMogul free?
faqs.answer: Yes, ChartMogul is free for companies tracking up to $10,000 in MRR; paid plans start once revenue exceeds that threshold.

faqs.question: What billing platforms does ChartMogul integrate with?
faqs.answer: ChartMogul integrates with more than 25 billing systems, including Stripe, Chargebee, Recurly, Braintree, PayPal, and Chargify.

faqs.question: How is ChartMogul pricing calculated?
faqs.answer: Starter and Pro plan prices scale with a company's ARR, ranging roughly from $59 to $1,199 per month; Enterprise starts at $19,900/year.

faqs.question: Who founded ChartMogul?
faqs.answer: ChartMogul was founded in 2014 by Nick Franklin, a former Zendesk director, in Berlin, Germany.

faqs.question: Does ChartMogul replace my billing system?
faqs.answer: No, ChartMogul connects to your existing billing system(s) as an analytics layer; it does not process payments itself.

enrichment.short_description: chasquid is a free, open-source SMTP email server written in Go, built for simplicity, secure defaults, and easy self-hosting on Linux.
enrichment.seo_title: chasquid: Open-Source Self-Hosted SMTP Server Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: chasquid is a free, open-source SMTP server written in Go for simple, secure self-hosted email. Learn its features, security model, and setup basics.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: chasquid is a free, open-source SMTP (email) server written in the Go programming language and released under the Apache License 2.0. It is developed and maintained by Alberto Bertogli (online handle "albertito") as an independent open-source project rather than a commercial company, with source code hosted on both GitLab and GitHub. chasquid functions as a Mail Transfer Agent (MTA) - the software that accepts, routes, and delivers email between mail servers - and positions itself as a simpler alternative to long-established MTAs such as Postfix and Exim. Its stated design goals are simplicity, secure-by-default behavior, and ease of operation, specifically targeting individuals and small groups running their own mail server rather than large enterprises or ISPs. The project emphasizes that it should be hard to misconfigure in harmful or insecure ways, for example by preventing open-relay configurations or unintentional clear-text authentication. Key technical features include virtual domain support with per-domain users and email aliases, suffix-dropping (routing user+tag@domain to user@domain), SPF and MTA-STS validation for anti-spoofing, DKIM signing and verification, TLS security-level tracking to detect and prevent encryption downgrade attacks, support for multiple TLS certificates on a single instance, and internationalized usernames and domain names (SMTPUTF8 and IDNA). chasquid integrates with Dovecot for authentication and provides hooks for external greylisting, antivirus, and antispam tools rather than reimplementing that functionality itself. It also ships a monitoring HTTP server that exposes internal variables and request tracing to aid debugging and observability. TLS certificate provisioning is designed to integrate easily with Let's Encrypt for free, automatically renewed certificates. As a single Go binary, chasquid has minimal runtime dependencies and is available as a packaged install on Debian, Ubuntu, Arch Linux, and Alpine Linux, in addition to being buildable from source. Configuration is handled through plain text files rather than a database or web UI. Because it is fully open source and self-hosted, there is no pricing, subscription, or vendor lock-in - operators run chasquid on their own server (a VPS, home server, or on-premise machine) and are responsible for their own uptime, backups, and DNS/MX record setup. chasquid is best suited to technically proficient users - developers, system administrators, and privacy-conscious individuals - who want full control over their email infrastructure and are comfortable working with configuration files and Linux administration, rather than users looking for a hosted, no-configuration email service. It does not provide a web-based inbox itself; it handles mail transport and is typically paired with an IMAP server such as Dovecot plus a separate webmail or desktop mail client. Recent releases, such as version 1.13, have continued to add security hardening, including protections against SMTP smuggling attacks and support for receive-only user accounts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: chasquid is a free, open-source SMTP email server written in Go and released under the Apache License 2.0. It is maintained by independent developer Alberto Bertogli and is designed as a simpler, more secure-by-default alternative to established mail transfer agents like Postfix and Exim.

The project targets individuals and small groups who want to self-host their own email infrastructure, prioritizing configurations that are hard to get wrong in insecure ways over the flexibility of a fully general-purpose MTA.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: chasquid supports virtual domains and aliases, DKIM signing and verification, SPF and MTA-STS validation, and TLS downgrade protection, along with internationalized usernames and domains (SMTPUTF8/IDNA).

It integrates with Dovecot for authentication, supports Let's Encrypt for automatic TLS certificates, and exposes a monitoring HTTP endpoint for debugging, while leaving spam and antivirus filtering to external hook-based tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: chasquid is completely free and open source with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or hosted offering from the maintainer. Users self-host it on their own infrastructure (a VPS or on-premise server) and are responsible for their own hosting costs, backups, and uptime.

features.title: Virtual domains and aliases
features.description: Per-domain users and email aliases, including automatic suffix-dropping for plus-addressing.

features.title: DKIM signing and verification
features.description: Built-in support for signing outgoing mail and verifying incoming DKIM signatures.

features.title: SPF and MTA-STS validation
features.description: Validates sender policies and enforces MTA-STS to reduce spoofing and downgrade attacks.

features.title: TLS downgrade protection
features.description: Tracks per-domain TLS security levels to detect and prevent encryption downgrades.

features.title: Let's Encrypt integration
features.description: Designed to work easily with Let's Encrypt for free, auto-renewed TLS certificates.

features.title: Dovecot authentication
features.description: Integrates with Dovecot for user authentication.

features.title: Monitoring HTTP server
features.description: Exposes internal variables and request tracing for debugging.

features.title: Internationalized addressing
features.description: Supports SMTPUTF8 and IDNA for non-ASCII usernames and domains.

pros: Completely free and fully open source
Secure-by-default configuration reduces common mail server misconfigurations
Lightweight single Go binary with minimal dependencies
Actively maintained with regular security-focused releases
cons: No graphical or web-based admin interface
Requires Linux system administration knowledge to set up and operate
No built-in webmail client or inbox; must be paired with a separate mail client
Smaller community and fewer tutorials than Postfix or Exim

faqs.question: What is chasquid?
faqs.answer: chasquid is a free, open-source SMTP email server written in Go, designed for simple and secure self-hosted email.

faqs.question: Is chasquid free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, chasquid is completely free and open source under the Apache License 2.0.

faqs.question: Does chasquid support DKIM and Let's Encrypt?
faqs.answer: Yes, chasquid supports DKIM signing/verification and is designed to integrate easily with Let's Encrypt for automatic TLS certificates.

faqs.question: Who maintains chasquid?
faqs.answer: chasquid is developed and maintained by Alberto Bertogli as an independent open-source project.

faqs.question: Can beginners self-host email with chasquid?
faqs.answer: chasquid is aimed at technically proficient users comfortable with Linux administration and configuration files; it has no graphical setup wizard.

faqs.question: Does chasquid include a webmail inbox?
faqs.answer: No, chasquid only handles mail transport (SMTP); it is typically paired with an IMAP server like Dovecot and a separate mail client.

enrichment.short_description: ChatGPT is OpenAI's AI chatbot and assistant for conversation, writing, coding, and research, available via web, mobile, and desktop apps with a public API.
enrichment.seo_title: ChatGPT Review 2026: Features, Pricing Plans & Models
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ChatGPT is OpenAI's AI assistant for chat, writing, and coding. Compare 2026 pricing (Free, Go, Plus, Pro, Business, Enterprise), features, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ChatGPT is an AI-powered chatbot and assistant developed by OpenAI, first launched to the public on November 30, 2022, built on OpenAI's GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) family of large language models. OpenAI was founded in December 2015 by Sam Altman, Elon Musk, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, and others as an AI research lab, and is headquartered in San Francisco, California; the company has grown to roughly 8,000 employees as of 2026. ChatGPT enables natural-language conversations with an AI model for tasks including writing and editing, coding and debugging, research and summarization, data analysis, image generation and analysis, and spoken voice conversation. It is available as a web app at chatgpt.com, as native iOS and Android mobile apps, and as desktop apps for macOS and Windows, alongside browser extensions and a developer API for embedding the underlying models into third-party products. As of mid-2026, ChatGPT runs on OpenAI's GPT-5 model family, which spans multiple capability tiers so that lightweight, low-latency variants handle everyday chat while higher-effort variants are reserved for complex reasoning, longer agentic tasks, and advanced coding; the specific model used for a given query depends on the user's plan and query complexity. ChatGPT's pricing follows a freemium, tiered structure. The Free plan gives limited access to the fast, default model with periodic message caps. ChatGPT Go, first launched in India in 2025 and rolled out worldwide by January 2026, is a lower-cost tier priced at roughly $8/month that raises free-tier message, upload, and image-generation limits. ChatGPT Plus, at $20/month, is the most popular individual paid tier and unlocks the flagship model, higher usage limits, image generation, voice mode, and features like Projects, Custom GPTs, and coding-agent tools. ChatGPT Pro, at $200/month, targets power users and professionals who need the largest usage allowances and access to the highest-effort reasoning models. For organizations, ChatGPT Business (the evolution of the earlier ChatGPT Team plan) is priced at roughly $25-30 per user per month and adds admin controls, higher usage limits, and workspace data excluded from model training by default; ChatGPT Enterprise uses custom, negotiated pricing and adds SSO, SCIM provisioning, enterprise security/compliance certifications, unlimited high-speed access, and dedicated support. Beyond plain chat, ChatGPT supports Custom GPTs (user-configured assistants with specific instructions, knowledge files, and tools), a Projects feature for organizing related conversations and files, persistent memory across sessions, real-time web browsing with citations, sandboxed code execution (Advanced Data Analysis/Code Interpreter), image generation, and Voice Mode. ChatGPT's main competitors include Google's Gemini, Anthropic's Claude, Microsoft Copilot, and Perplexity. It remains the highest-traffic consumer AI assistant globally and is commonly cited as one of the fastest-adopted consumer software products in history, with hundreds of millions of weekly active users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ChatGPT is OpenAI's conversational AI assistant, launched publicly on November 30, 2022. OpenAI itself was founded in 2015 by Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, and others, and is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with roughly 8,000 employees as of 2026.

ChatGPT is used for writing, coding, research, data analysis, and conversation, and is available on the web, iOS and Android apps, macOS and Windows desktop apps, and via a developer API. As of mid-2026 it runs on OpenAI's GPT-5 model family, which spans multiple capability tiers routed by plan and task complexity.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include Custom GPTs (configurable assistants with their own instructions and knowledge), Projects for organizing related work, persistent memory across conversations, real-time web browsing with citations, sandboxed code execution, image generation, and Voice Mode for spoken conversation.

Developers can build on ChatGPT's underlying models through OpenAI's API, and organizations can deploy ChatGPT with admin controls, SSO, and data-training exclusions through the Business and Enterprise tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ChatGPT offers a Free plan, a lower-cost Go plan (~$8/month), Plus ($20/month), and Pro ($200/month) for individuals, plus Business (~$25-30/user/month) and custom-priced Enterprise plans for organizations. Higher tiers add usage headroom, advanced model access, and administrative/security controls rather than a fundamentally different product.

features.title: GPT-5 model family access
features.description: Conversational AI spanning fast everyday models to higher-effort reasoning models for complex tasks.

features.title: Voice Mode
features.description: Natural spoken conversation with the AI assistant.

features.title: Custom GPTs
features.description: User-configured assistants with custom instructions, knowledge files, and tools.

features.description: Live web search with cited sources for up-to-date answers.

features.description: Built-in AI image generation from text prompts.

features.title: Developer API
features.description: Programmatic access to OpenAI's models for building custom applications.

pros: Extremely capable general-purpose AI assistant for writing, coding, and research
Usable free tier with no cost to get started
Available across web, mobile, and desktop platforms
Fast-moving feature set including voice, agents, and coding tools
cons: Free tier has tight usage limits compared to paid plans
Pro tier is expensive at $200/month
Outputs can be inaccurate or hallucinate and require human verification
Enterprise-grade features require a custom sales conversation rather than self-serve signup

faqs.question: Is ChatGPT free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, ChatGPT has a Free plan with limited usage; paid plans (Go, Plus, Pro, Business, Enterprise) offer higher limits and more capable models.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ChatGPT Plus and Pro?
faqs.answer: Plus ($20/month) gives most users full access to the flagship model with generous usage limits; Pro ($200/month) provides much higher usage headroom and access to the most capable reasoning models for power users.

faqs.question: What AI model does ChatGPT use?
faqs.answer: As of mid-2026, ChatGPT runs on OpenAI's GPT-5 model family, which includes multiple capability tiers for everyday chat versus complex reasoning and coding tasks.

faqs.question: Can I use ChatGPT for coding?
faqs.answer: Yes, ChatGPT can write, explain, and debug code, and Plus/Pro/Business/Enterprise plans include access to coding-agent tools.

faqs.question: Does OpenAI use my ChatGPT conversations to train its models?
faqs.answer: Consumer plan usage may be used to improve models unless a user opts out in settings; Business and Enterprise plans exclude workspace data from training by default.

faqs.question: Is there a ChatGPT API?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenAI offers a separate, usage-based developer API that gives programmatic access to the models behind ChatGPT.

use_cases.title: Everyday writing and research

use_cases.title: Software development assistance
use_cases.description: Write, explain, debug, and refactor code across many programming languages.

use_cases.description: Roll out a secure, admin-controlled AI assistant across an organization with SSO and data protections.

features: Access to most capable reasoning models

enrichment.short_description: Chatmeter is an AI-powered reputation and review management platform that helps multi-location brands monitor, respond to, and analyze customer feedback.
enrichment.seo_title: Chatmeter Review 2026: Reputation Mgmt Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chatmeter is an AI-powered review and reputation management platform for multi-location brands. See 2026 features, pricing model, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chatmeter is an AI-powered brand reputation and local listings management platform built for multi-location businesses such as retail chains, restaurant groups, healthcare networks, and franchises. The company was founded in 2009 in San Diego, California by Collin Holmes as a local search marketing solution and grew into a reputation and review intelligence platform serving brands managing dozens to thousands of physical locations; it has roughly 51-200 employees. In September 2025, Chatmeter was acquired by Alchemer, a customer experience and feedback technology company based in Louisville, Colorado, from growth equity firm PSG. Chatmeter continues to operate as a distinct product line integrated into Alchemer's broader CX platform, with its team remaining based in San Diego. Chatmeter's core function is aggregating and analyzing reviews, ratings, and social mentions from Google, Yelp, Facebook, TripAdvisor, and dozens of other review and social platforms into a single unified inbox, so location and marketing managers do not need to log into each site separately. It uses AI to draft suggested responses to reviews that managers can review and approve before publishing, which saves time for teams managing review volume across many locations. Sentiment analysis surfaces recurring themes in customer feedback - for example, flagging that a particular complaint is rising at a subset of locations - helping brands prioritize operational fixes. Chatmeter also manages local business listings and location data across directories to keep name, address, and phone (NAP) information consistent for local SEO, and it offers location-specific landing pages that can include structured data (schema markup), promotions, and location-specific content to support local search visibility. Because reviews and local listing accuracy are ranking factors for local search and map-pack results, Chatmeter positions itself as both a customer-experience tool and a local SEO tool. The platform reports having analyzed data across more than 1.9 million locations and billions of reviews, social mentions, and sentiment data points since its founding. Chatmeter does not publish self-serve pricing; the company prices by number of managed locations with unlimited user seats included, and prospective customers go through a demo and custom proposal process rather than choosing from listed plans, making it best suited to regional chains, franchise networks, and enterprise brands with roughly 50 or more locations rather than single-location small businesses. Competitors in the reputation and local-listings management space include Birdeye, Podium, Yext, and Reputation.com. Chatmeter differentiates itself through its focus on large, multi-location brands and its combination of review management, sentiment analytics, and local SEO/listings tools in one platform - a positioning that Alchemer's acquisition is intended to extend by pairing Chatmeter's indirect feedback (public reviews, social mentions) with Alchemer's direct feedback tools (surveys, in-app prompts).

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Chatmeter is a reputation and local listings management platform founded in 2009 in San Diego, California by Collin Holmes. It focuses on multi-location businesses - retail chains, restaurant groups, healthcare networks, and franchises - that need to manage reviews and local search presence across dozens or thousands of locations.

In September 2025, Chatmeter was acquired by Alchemer, a customer experience and feedback technology company, from growth equity firm PSG. Chatmeter continues to operate under its own brand with its team based in San Diego, now integrated into Alchemer's broader CX platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Chatmeter aggregates reviews from Google, Yelp, Facebook, TripAdvisor, and other platforms into a unified inbox, with AI-drafted review responses that managers approve before publishing, and sentiment analysis that identifies recurring feedback themes across locations.
It also manages local business listings for NAP consistency and offers location-specific landing pages with schema markup to support local SEO alongside reputation management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Chatmeter does not publish self-serve pricing. It prices by number of managed locations with unlimited included user seats, and prospective customers go through a demo and custom proposal process. It is best suited to brands managing roughly 50 or more locations.

cons: Pricing is not published and requires a sales demo/proposal
Not cost-effective for single-location small businesses

faqs.question: What does Chatmeter do?
faqs.answer: Chatmeter aggregates and manages online reviews, local listings, and social mentions for multi-location brands, using AI to help draft review responses and surface sentiment trends.

faqs.question: How much does Chatmeter cost?
faqs.answer: Chatmeter does not publish set pricing; it charges by number of managed locations with unlimited user seats, quoted through a demo and proposal process.

faqs.question: Is Chatmeter part of Alchemer now?
faqs.answer: Yes, Alchemer acquired Chatmeter in September 2025; Chatmeter continues operating as its own product line within Alchemer's customer experience platform.

faqs.question: What review platforms does Chatmeter monitor?
faqs.answer: Chatmeter monitors Google, Yelp, Facebook, TripAdvisor, and dozens of other review and social platforms.

faqs.question: Who is Chatmeter best suited for?
faqs.answer: Regional chains, franchise networks, and enterprise brands managing roughly 50 or more physical locations.

faqs.question: Does Chatmeter use AI to respond to reviews?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chatmeter generates AI-drafted review responses that managers can review and approve before they are published.

enrichment.short_description: Chatsonic is Writesonic's AI chatbot offering real-time web search grounding, multi-model access (GPT, Claude, Gemini), and built-in image generation.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chatsonic is Writesonic's AI chatbot with real-time search and multi-model access. Compare 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs before signing up.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chatsonic is an AI chatbot product built by Writesonic, a company founded in October 2020 by Samanyou Garg and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Chatsonic launched on December 12, 2022, shortly after ChatGPT's public debut, and was originally positioned as a ChatGPT alternative that addressed several of ChatGPT's early limitations at the time - most notably by adding real-time internet access via Google Search integration so it could answer questions about current events and recent data, along with built-in AI image generation and voice input/output. Since its 2022 launch, Chatsonic has evolved from a standalone chatbot into a multi-model AI assistant embedded within Writesonic's broader platform: rather than being limited to a single underlying model, Chatsonic lets users switch between models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google within the same chat interface, along with Flux-based image generation. It also offers configurable persona modes intended to adapt tone and behavior for different use cases, such as students drafting essays, marketers writing copy, or developers debugging code. Writesonic itself has broadened its positioning over time from an AI writing and content-generation tool into what it now describes as an AI search visibility and generative engine optimization (GEO) platform, adding features that track how a brand or website is represented in AI-generated answers from systems like ChatGPT and Perplexity, in addition to its original long-form content and copywriting tools; Chatsonic sits within this broader Writesonic product suite as its conversational AI interface. Pricing for Chatsonic/Writesonic is tiered: an Individual plan is priced at roughly $20 per month, providing access to Chatsonic's real-time-search chatbot along with core Writesonic features. Higher tiers aimed at teams and larger content/SEO programs are priced substantially higher, with reported figures placing a team-level Basic plan around $249/month and a Growth-level plan around $499/month, reflecting higher usage limits for content generation, GEO tracking, and multi-seat access; exact tier names and included credits should be confirmed on Writesonic's pricing page, since they change fairly often. Chatsonic/Writesonic competes with general AI chatbots like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini, as well as with AI writing tools like Jasper and Copy.ai, and increasingly with GEO/AI-search-visibility tools. Its main differentiators versus a plain chatbot are bundled real-time search grounding, multi-model switching in one interface, and integration with Writesonic's content and SEO/GEO tooling, making it most relevant to marketers, content teams, and SEO practitioners who want an AI chat interface alongside content production and AI-search-visibility tracking, rather than users who simply want a general-purpose assistant.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Chatsonic is the AI chatbot product from Writesonic, a company founded in 2020 by Samanyou Garg and headquartered in San Francisco. Chatsonic launched in December 2022 as a ChatGPT alternative distinguished by real-time web search grounding and built-in image generation.

It has since evolved into a multi-model chat interface within Writesonic's broader content and generative-engine-optimization (GEO) platform, letting users switch between models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google in one place.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Chatsonic offers real-time internet search grounding for current-events questions, Flux-based AI image generation, voice input/output, and configurable persona modes for different use cases like marketing copy or coding help.

It's bundled with Writesonic's broader suite of content generation and AI search visibility (GEO) tools, so marketing and SEO teams can move between chat, content drafting, and AI-search-tracking features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Individual plan is priced at roughly $20/month. Team-oriented plans for larger content and GEO programs are priced substantially higher, with reported tiers around $249/month and $499/month depending on usage limits and seats.

faqs.question: What is Chatsonic?
faqs.answer: Chatsonic is Writesonic's AI chatbot, offering real-time web search grounding, multi-model access, and built-in image generation.

faqs.question: Is Chatsonic free?
faqs.answer: Chatsonic offers limited free access through Writesonic, with the Individual paid plan starting at roughly $20/month for full features.

faqs.question: What AI models does Chatsonic use?
faqs.answer: Chatsonic lets users switch between models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google within the same chat interface.

faqs.question: How is Chatsonic different from ChatGPT?
faqs.answer: Unlike a single-model chatbot, Chatsonic combines real-time search grounding, multiple AI models, and Writesonic's content/GEO tools in one product.

faqs.question: Who makes Chatsonic?
faqs.answer: Chatsonic is made by Writesonic, a company founded in 2020 by Samanyou Garg and headquartered in San Francisco.

faqs.question: Does Chatsonic have real-time internet access?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chatsonic can search the web in real time to answer questions about current events and recent data.

enrichment.short_description: Chatwoot is an open-source, AI-powered customer engagement platform for omnichannel live chat, email, and social support — self-hosted or cloud.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open-source / Freemium (self-hosted free, or per-agent subscription)
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, USA (engineering team based in Bengaluru, India)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chatwoot is an open-source customer support platform: self-host for free or use the cloud. See 2026 pricing plans, features, and FAQs before you choose.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chatwoot is an open-source customer engagement and support platform that provides live chat, email, and omnichannel messaging (WhatsApp, Instagram, Facebook, Telegram, Line, SMS, and a general API channel) in a single shared agent inbox. It was founded in 2017 by Pranav Raj S, Sojan Jose, and Nithin David Thomas, evolved from an earlier internal tool built in 2016, and was released as open-source software under the MIT license in 2019. The company went through Y Combinator's Winter 2021 batch and operates with a small, distributed team of roughly 10-15 people, with engineering concentrated in Bengaluru, India, alongside a US business presence. Chatwoot markets itself as an open-source alternative to commercial customer support platforms such as Intercom, Zendesk, and Salesforce Service Cloud. Its architecture is built on Ruby on Rails with PostgreSQL and Redis, typically deployed with Docker, which lets technical teams self-host the entire platform on their own infrastructure for full data ownership and compliance control - the self-hosted Community Edition is free and open source with no per-agent licensing fee. For teams that don't want to manage their own servers, Chatwoot also offers a hosted cloud version with tiered, per-agent pricing: Hacker is free for up to 2 agents and 500 conversations per month with live chat and core features; Startups is $19/agent/month (billed annually) and unlocks unlimited conversations across all channels except voice, a help center, custom views, and 300 monthly Captain AI credits; Business is $39/agent/month (billed annually, the most popular cloud tier) and adds voice channel support, team-based routing, automation rules, campaigns, CSAT surveys, SLAs, and 500 Captain AI credits; and Enterprise is $99/agent/month (billed annually) and adds SSO/SAML, audit logs, agent capacity management, white-labeling, and 800 Captain AI credits. Paid self-hosted plans with vendor-managed maintenance are also available in a similar $19-99/agent/month range for organizations that want Chatwoot's premium features while keeping data on their own servers. Captain is Chatwoot's built-in AI agent, which can auto-respond to common customer queries, summarize conversations, and suggest replies to human agents; additional Captain AI credits beyond a plan's monthly allotment cost $20 per 1,000 credits. Other core features include automated assignment rules and canned responses, contact profiles with full conversation history, internal notes and @mentions for team collaboration, a reporting suite, and integrations such as Slack and Dialogflow. Because it is open source, Chatwoot has an active developer community that contributes translations, integrations, and self-hosting guides, and it is commonly chosen by privacy-conscious companies, budget-constrained startups, and organizations in regulated industries that need to keep customer data within their own infrastructure rather than a third-party SaaS vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Chatwoot is an open-source customer engagement platform founded in 2017 by Pranav Raj S, Sojan Jose, and Nithin David Thomas, and open-sourced under the MIT license in 2019 after going through Y Combinator's Winter 2021 batch. It provides an alternative to commercial platforms like Intercom and Zendesk that can be self-hosted or used as a managed cloud service.

Built on Ruby on Rails, PostgreSQL, and Redis, and typically deployed via Docker, Chatwoot lets teams either run the software entirely on their own infrastructure for full data control, or use Chatwoot's hosted cloud plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Chatwoot unifies live chat, email, and channels like WhatsApp, Instagram, Facebook, and Telegram into one shared inbox, with automation rules, canned responses, and internal team collaboration via notes and @mentions.
Captain, Chatwoot's built-in AI agent, can auto-respond to common queries, summarize conversations, and suggest replies, with usage measured in monthly AI credits that vary by plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The self-hosted Community Edition is free and open source with no per-agent fee. Chatwoot's cloud plans are billed per agent per month: Hacker is free for up to 2 agents, Startups is $19/agent/month, Business is $39/agent/month, and Enterprise is $99/agent/month, all billed annually. Paid, vendor-managed self-hosted plans are available in a similar price range.

features.title: Omnichannel shared inbox
features.description: Manage live chat, email, WhatsApp, Instagram, Facebook, Telegram, and more from one inbox.

features.title: Self-hosted Community Edition
features.description: Fully open-source, free-to-self-host version under the MIT license.

features.title: Captain AI agent
features.description: Built-in AI that auto-responds to common queries, summarizes conversations, and suggests replies.

features.title: Automation rules
features.description: Auto-assign agents, send canned responses, and tag conversations based on rules.

features.title: Help center
features.description: Self-service knowledge base for customers, included on paid plans.

features.title: Team collaboration tools
features.description: Internal private notes and @mentions for agent coordination.

features.title: Reporting suite
features.description: Dashboards and reports on conversation volume, response times, and CSAT.

features.title: SSO/SAML and audit logs
features.description: Enterprise-tier security and compliance controls.

pros: Free, fully open-source self-hosting option with no per-agent fee
Full data ownership and control, useful for compliance-sensitive organizations
Transparent, straightforward per-agent cloud pricing
Active open-source community contributing integrations and translations
cons: Self-hosting requires DevOps effort to manage Rails, PostgreSQL, Redis, and Docker
Voice channel and several advanced features are limited to Business/Enterprise tiers
Captain AI usage is credit-limited, with overage credits billed separately
Smaller third-party integration ecosystem than larger platforms like Zendesk or Intercom

faqs.question: Is Chatwoot really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted Community Edition is completely free and open source under the MIT license; Chatwoot also offers a free Hacker cloud plan for up to 2 agents.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Chatwoot?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chatwoot can be self-hosted on your own infrastructure using Docker, giving you full control over your customer data.

faqs.question: What is Captain in Chatwoot?
faqs.answer: Captain is Chatwoot's built-in AI agent that can auto-respond to common queries, summarize conversations, and suggest replies to human agents.

faqs.question: What channels does Chatwoot support?
faqs.answer: Chatwoot supports live chat, email, WhatsApp, Instagram, Facebook, Telegram, Line, SMS, voice (on higher tiers), and a general API channel.

faqs.question: How does Chatwoot cloud pricing work?
faqs.answer: Cloud pricing is billed per agent per month, ranging from a free Hacker plan up to $99/agent/month on Enterprise, all billed annually.

faqs.question: Who founded Chatwoot?
faqs.answer: Chatwoot was founded in 2017 by Pranav Raj S, Sojan Jose, and Nithin David Thomas, and went through Y Combinator's Winter 2021 batch.

use_cases.description: Migrate from Intercom or Zendesk to an open-source alternative with comparable features.

enrichment.short_description: Checkly is a developer-first monitoring platform combining Playwright-native browser checks and API monitoring to catch issues before users do.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Checkly is a Playwright-native synthetic monitoring platform for APIs and browser flows. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Checkly is a developer-first synthetic monitoring platform founded in 2018 by Hannes Lenke and Tim Nolet, headquartered in Berlin, Germany, with a distributed team of roughly 50-58 people across multiple continents. The company has raised approximately $32 million in total funding, including a $20 million Series B in 2024 led by Balderton Capital with participation from Accel and CRV. Checkly's product lets engineering teams monitor the availability and correctness of websites, APIs, and critical user flows by running scheduled checks - API checks (HTTP request assertions) and browser checks (full user-flow simulations) - from up to 22 global locations, as often as every minute. Its key differentiator is being built natively on Playwright, Microsoft's open-source browser automation framework: rather than requiring a proprietary scripting language, Checkly lets teams write monitoring checks as real Playwright test code and reuse existing end-to-end test suites as production monitors, avoiding duplicate test and monitoring codebases. Checkly follows a monitoring-as-code philosophy, so checks are defined in code via its CLI and constructs library and deployed through CI/CD pipelines and version control rather than being configured only through a web UI, appealing to engineering teams that want monitoring managed the same way as application code. In early 2026, Checkly introduced Rocky, an AI agent that automatically triages check failures, analyzes network traces and traceroutes, and surfaces likely root causes to reduce manual incident investigation time. The platform reports running roughly 32.5 million checks per day for over 1,000 customers, including companies such as Vercel, 1Password, and Autodesk. Checkly's pricing is usage-based and tiered: a free Hobby plan includes 10 uptime monitors, 1,000 browser check runs, 10,000 API check runs per month, 6 monitoring locations, and 1 user, with no credit card required to start; the Starter plan is priced around $24/month (billed annually) and raises limits to 50 uptime monitors, 3,000 browser checks, 25,000 API checks, and 3 users; the Team plan is priced around $64/month (billed annually) and adds all 22 global monitoring locations, 10 users, traceroute monitoring, private locations, role-based access control, and public status pages; and Enterprise pricing is custom, adding unlimited users, dedicated support, and higher volume allowances. Overage pricing applies beyond each plan's included volume - for example, additional browser check runs cost around $4 per 1,000 runs. Checkly is aimed at software engineering and platform/SRE teams who want to monitor both the frontend user experience and backend API dependencies from a single tool with a code-first workflow, and it competes with tools like Datadog Synthetics, Better Stack, Pingdom, UptimeRobot, and New Relic Synthetics, differentiating primarily through its Playwright-native approach and generous free tier for individual developers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Checkly is a synthetic monitoring platform founded in 2018 by Hannes Lenke and Tim Nolet, headquartered in Berlin, Germany. The company has raised roughly $32 million in funding, including a 2024 Series B led by Balderton Capital, and employs around 50-58 people.

Checkly runs scheduled API and browser checks from up to 22 global locations to monitor the uptime and correctness of websites and APIs, built natively on Microsoft's Playwright framework so teams can reuse existing end-to-end tests as production monitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Checkly combines Playwright-powered browser checks with HTTP API checks, both defined as code and deployed through CI/CD pipelines under a monitoring-as-code philosophy, along with public status pages and alerting integrations.

In early 2026, Checkly added Rocky, an AI agent that automatically triages check failures and analyzes network traces to surface likely root causes, reducing manual investigation time during incidents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Checkly offers a free Hobby plan (10 uptime monitors, 1,000 browser checks, 10,000 API checks/month). Paid plans scale usage and locations: Starter is around $24/month and Team is around $64/month, both billed annually, with custom Enterprise pricing for larger organizations.

features.title: Playwright-native browser checks
features.description: Write monitoring checks as real Playwright test code and reuse existing E2E suites as monitors.

features.title: API monitoring
features.description: HTTP request checks with assertions on status, headers, and response body.

features.title: Monitoring as code
features.description: Define and deploy checks via CLI and CI/CD pipelines instead of a web UI only.

features.title: 22 global monitoring locations
features.description: Run checks from up to 22 locations worldwide to catch regional issues.

features.title: Rocky AI triage agent
features.description: AI agent that automatically triages failures and analyzes traces to surface root causes.

features.title: Public status pages
features.description: Communicate uptime and incident status to users and customers.

features.title: Traceroute diagnostics
features.description: Network-level diagnostics for troubleshooting connectivity issues.

features.title: Alerting integrations
features.description: Notify teams via Slack, PagerDuty, and other channels when checks fail.

pros: Generous free Hobby tier with no credit card required
Reuses existing Playwright test suites as production monitors
Code-first workflow fits naturally into modern CI/CD pipelines
AI-assisted failure triage (Rocky) reduces incident investigation time
cons: All 22 monitoring locations, RBAC, and status pages require the Team tier or above
Overage charges can add up at high check volumes
Full value requires familiarity with Playwright and code-based configuration
Enterprise pricing is not publicly listed

faqs.question: What is Checkly used for?
faqs.answer: Checkly is used to monitor the uptime and correctness of websites, APIs, and critical user flows through scheduled browser and API checks.

faqs.question: Is Checkly free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Checkly offers a free Hobby plan with 10 uptime monitors, 1,000 browser checks, and 10,000 API checks per month, no credit card required.

faqs.question: Does Checkly require Playwright knowledge?
faqs.answer: Checkly is built natively on Playwright, so writing custom browser checks benefits from Playwright familiarity, though basic API checks can be set up without it.

faqs.question: What is Rocky in Checkly?
faqs.answer: Rocky is Checkly's AI agent, introduced in early 2026, that automatically triages check failures and analyzes network traces to surface likely root causes.

faqs.question: How many monitoring locations does Checkly offer?
faqs.answer: Checkly can run checks from up to 22 global locations, though all locations are only included on the Team plan and above.

faqs.question: Who founded Checkly?
faqs.answer: Checkly was founded in 2018 by Hannes Lenke and Tim Nolet, and is headquartered in Berlin, Germany.

use_cases.title: Uptime and API monitoring
use_cases.description: Continuously monitor critical APIs and endpoints and get alerted the moment something breaks.

use_cases.title: Reusing E2E tests as monitors
use_cases.description: Turn existing Playwright end-to-end test suites into scheduled production monitors without duplicate code.

use_cases.title: Global synthetic monitoring for SRE teams
use_cases.description: Monitor user-facing flows from multiple global regions with alerting and status pages.
